retrieved_docs.title: Ketamine Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Ketamine is a structural analog of the dissociative anesthetic and recreational drug phencyclidine (PCP). Similar to phencyclidine, ketamine causes analgesia and amnesia without the cardiovascular and respiratory depression associated with common anesthetics. Originally called CI-581, ketamine has one-tenth the potency of PCP and causes less severe dysphoria and hallucinations. Intramuscular and intravenous forms of ketamine are commonly used to provide pediatric anesthesia, especially for high-risk children or patients in limited-resource settings. In surgical settings, ketamine is typically combined with benzodiazepines, which can reduce the adverse psychological symptoms that occur during emergence. Off-label, subanesthetic doses of ketamine also have a use for acute and chronic pain management, sedation, and treatment of severe depression. Like its chemical cousin phencyclidine, ketamine’s psychomimetic effects have made it a popular recreational drug. In low doses, its euphoric and dissociative effects are sometimes referred to as “k-land,” whereas at high doses, the immobilizing and hallucinogenic effects are referred to as being in a “k-hole.” In the context of an illegal, recreational drug, ketamine goes by the street names “K,” “vitamin K,” “super K,” “special K,” “super C,” “special LA coke,” “jet,” “superacid,” and “green.” Ketamine toxicity can cause a variety of neurological, cardiovascular, psychiatric, urogenital, and abdominal symptoms, which are dose-dependent, and whether ketamine administration was in an iatrogenic or illicit context. For example, some experts have attributed the higher incidence of ulcerative cystitis in recreational users to the adulterants with which the drug is mixed. Providers using ketamine should be aware of the various mechanisms to treat ketamine toxicity and to prevent acute complications such as rhabdomyolysis and seizures, and chronic complications such as psychiatric disturbances and ulcerative cystitis. This activity reviews the evaluation of ketamine toxicity and the role of the interprofessional team in managing this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Brief Psychotic Disorder -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Brief psychotic disorder (BPD) according to DSM-5 is the sudden onset of psychotic behavior that lasts less than 1 month followed by complete remission with possible future relapses. It is differentiated from schizophreniform disorder and schizophrenia by the duration of the psychosis. The diagnosis is often anticipatory or retrospective due to the diagnostic requirement of complete remission within 1 month. This activity describes the presentation and pathophysiology of brief psychotic disorder and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review risk factors for shared psychotic disorder. Describe the pathophysiology of shared psychotic disorder. Describe the importance of collaboration and communication amongst an interprofessional team to improve outcomes for those affected by shared psychotic disorder. retrieved_docs.title: Delusional Misidentification Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education -- Family Education and Counseling
retrieved_docs.content: In a 2-year epidemiological survey regarding violence in first-break psychosis, authors postulated a high-risk triad of valued beliefs (ie, anger, being spied on, and conspiracy) mediated by anger. They identified targets as risk factors for aggression and violence. [53] Close relatives or attachment figures have been established as having significantly higher target risk. Prompt identification of medical and psychiatric decompensation is paramount to avoid detrimental effects on quality of life and potential target violence.

retrieved_docs.title: Paranoid Personality Disorder -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: PPD typically begins in early adulthood, often leading to increased risks of depressive and anxiety disorders. The severity of paranoia can result in impulsivity, aggression, grudge-bearing, and over-defensiveness. The diagnosis of PPD is made using the criteria described in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5-TR). This disorder can adversely affect multiple aspects of life, underscoring the need for early intervention and interdisciplinary care. This activity describes the epidemiology, genetics, clinical presentation, and treatment of PPD, providing healthcare professionals with the knowledge and tools to improve patient care for this complex condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Brief Psychotic Disorder -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Reliable data on the frequency of brief psychotic disorder are not available, mostly because of its low incidence and variation based on the population under study. However, increased frequency of the disorder generally occurs in populations known to be under high stress such as immigrants, refugees, earthquake victims, etc. [4] [5] A study researching the Finnish population found the prevalence of brief psychotic disorder to be 0.05%. [6] Another study in rural Ireland found 10 cases of BPD among 196 first-admission psychosis cases. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Schizophrenia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Schizophrenia, a serious mental illness, affects 1% of the global population and is marked by hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech, grossly disorganized behavior, and negative signs and symptoms such as reduced emotional expression, avolition, and cognitive impairment. Symptoms generally emerge in late adolescence or early adulthood, and the disorder may be more common in men. Causes include genetic, environmental, and neurobiological factors. No single gene is responsible; there is an interplay of multiple genetic factors. Environmental influences include prenatal and obstetrical risks, psychosocial stressors, and cannabis use. Neurobiologically, schizophrenia is associated with neurotransmitter dysfunction in dopamine and glutamate systems, as well as brain structural changes. Individualized comprehensive management plans, including antipsychotic medications for positive symptoms and evidence-based psychosocial interventions, are essential for improving patient quality of life. This activity describes the epidemiology, genetics, clinical presentation, and management of schizophrenia, providing healthcare professionals with the knowledge and tools to improve patient care for this complex and disabling condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)” is presented as a broad grouping that encompasses a variety of psychotic and affective conditions. Within this group are several specific entities: “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active), “هوس شَبَقِيّ” (schizotypal mania), and “عاطفة متقلصة” (blunted affect). In addition to primary psychotic disorders, the SMQ grouping also includes mood‑related diagnoses such as “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة” (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة” (major depressive disorder, single episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة” (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), “اكتئاب أَوبِيّ” (atypical depression), and “التقلقل الوجداني” (affective lability). This reflects an integrated view in which depressive disorders and affective symptoms are considered part of the broader psychosis spectrum, especially when they may present with psychotic features. The entity “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” is also linked to the more general category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy,” indicating that this form of childhood psychosis is typically identified early in life. Furthermore, “هوس شَبَقِيّ” is related to “ذُهان عضوي” (organic psychosis) through a broader‑relationship link, suggesting that schizotypal mania can be viewed as a subtype or manifestation of an organic psychotic process. Overall, the SMQ framework integrates early‑onset psychotic conditions, organic psychoses, specific manic presentations, and a range of depressive and affective disorders, highlighting the clinical overlap among psychotic and mood disturbances and underscoring the importance of considering these interrelationships in diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 962
retrieved_docs.content: The category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy” has a broader relationship with ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active). This specific psychotic condition is a member of the larger classification الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) (psychosis and psychotic disorders). Within the الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) group, several mood‑related diagnoses are also listed as members: اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة (major depressive disorder, single episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), اكتئاب أَوبِيّ (atypical depression), and the symptom التقلقل الوجداني (mood lability). Moreover, the entire الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) category is itself a member of عاطفة متقلصة (blunted affect). This network of relationships illustrates the overlap between early‑onset psychotic conditions, broader psychotic disorder classifications, depressive mood disorders, and affective symptoms such as blunted affect, emphasizing the need for integrated assessment and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders serve as a classification for حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة, which are transient organic psychotic conditions. Recognizing that حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة fall under anxiety disorders can help clinicians appropriately assess and manage patients who present with transient psychotic features in the context of anxiety‑related pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 677
retrieved_docs.content: Transient organic psychotic conditions (حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة) are recognized as a subtype of organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي) and are classified within both anxiety disorders and mood disorders. The diagnostic category “Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere” is linked as a child of both mood disorders and anxiety disorders, indicating that it shares features of each. The depressive type of transient organic psychotic condition (Transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type) is closely related to mood disorders and may be considered synonymous with mood‑disorder presentations. Together, these relationships illustrate that certain transient mental disorders span the domains of anxiety, mood, and organic psychosis, highlighting the importance of an integrated diagnostic approach for accurate classification and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 956
retrieved_docs.content: Mental symptom is a broad clinical concept that includes diverse manifestations such as personality problems, loss of pleasure (anhedonia), and self‑destructive behavior. These mental symptoms are linked to psychotic conditions; specifically, they have a broader relationship with transient organic psychotic states (حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة). Transient organic psychotic states are a subtype of organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي), which itself is broadly related to schizophrenic delusion (هوس شَبَقِيّ). The overarching category of psychosis (الذهان) therefore encompasses transient organic psychotic states, situating them within the spectrum of organic psychosis and its association with schizophrenic delusional phenomena. This integrated view highlights how specific mental symptoms connect to distinct forms of organic psychosis and their broader psychotic correlates.

Alcohol‑related disorders are classified as an inverse subclass of substance‑use disorder (اضطراب تعاطي المواد الإدمانية). Both recurrent and single‑episode major depressive episodes are also placed within the psychosis and psychotic disorders grouping, highlighting the potential overlap of depressive and psychotic features and the intricate interplay between organic, psychotic, and mood‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: Adolescent mental disorder is conceptualized as a pathological developmental process that may evolve into a solitary, aggressive‑type behavior disorder, linking early‑life mental disturbances to severe behavioral dysregulation. Core features of this pathway—self‑destructive behavior, anhedonia, and personality‑related problems—are associated with transient organic psychotic states (حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة) classified under organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي), which encompasses non‑infectious encephalitis, psychosis and psychotic disorders, and non‑infectious encephalopathy/delirium. In the diagnostic hierarchy, oppositional defiant disorder is coded under “Other and unspecified mental conditions,” while attention‑deficit presentations fall under “Preadult mental disorders” and are inversely linked to the solitary, aggressive‑type behavior disorder, positioning attentional difficulties opposite oppositional defiant disorder.
